items.prompt: A class discussion presents a digital sharing, messaging, or online participation scenario. Show how the learner would identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways.
items.expected_evidence: identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways with bounded and technically valid computing reasoning.

items.prompt: A learner must decide how to credit a source, communicate to an audience, or respond to a computing-impact case. Show how they would connect a technical design or deployment choice to downstream social consequences.
items.expected_evidence: connect a technical design or deployment choice to downstream social consequences
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates connect a technical design or deployment choice to downstream social consequences with bounded and technically valid computing reasoning.

items.prompt: A school or community technology policy case raises questions about bias, privacy, or governance. Show how the learner would name a governance, accountability, or oversight mechanism relevant to the case.
items.expected_evidence: name a governance, accountability, or oversight mechanism relevant to the case
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates name a governance, accountability, or oversight mechanism relevant to the case with bounded and technically valid computing reasoning.

items.prompt: Why is computing ethics weaker when treated as personal opinion alone?
items.expected_evidence: because disciplined ethical analysis should use stakeholders, consequences, and accountability criteria rather than unsupported preference only.
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner explains the structure coherently and ties the explanation back to because disciplined ethical analysis should use stakeholders, consequences, and accountability criteria rather than unsupported preference only.

items.prompt: How can a technical system choice create a social consequence?
items.expected_evidence: a bounded design or deployment choice can change who is affected, what risks appear, or how decisions are made in the real world.
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner explains the structure coherently and ties the explanation back to a bounded design or deployment choice can change who is affected, what risks appear, or how decisions are made in the real world.

items.prompt: In a digital communication or computing-impact case, a learner responds in a way that suggests this breakdown: learner treats computing ethics as personal feeling without explicit criteria, stakeholders, or accountability reasoning. Diagnose the ethical or participation mistake. Focus on ethics-as-opinion only.
items.expected_evidence: identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner identifies the dominant breakdown, names the structural issue accurately, and redirects the work toward identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways.

items.prompt: In a digital communication or computing-impact case, a learner responds in a way that suggests this breakdown: learner discusses social impact without connecting it to a bounded technical system choice. Diagnose the ethical or participation mistake. Focus on technical-social disconnect.
items.expected_evidence: connect a technical design or deployment choice to downstream social consequences
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner identifies the dominant breakdown, names the structural issue accurately, and redirects the work toward connect a technical design or deployment choice to downstream social consequences.

items.prompt: A digital citizenship or ethics idea learned in one classroom example is now presented in a less-familiar platform or public-systems context. Explain how the learner would name a governance, accountability, or oversight mechanism relevant to the case.
items.expected_evidence: name a governance, accountability, or oversight mechanism relevant to the case
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner preserves name a governance, accountability, or oversight mechanism relevant to the case in a changed computing context or representation.

items.prompt: A learner must move from a personal-use case to a broader social or institutional computing case and still preserve the same impact reasoning. Show how they would avoid collapsing ethical analysis into unsupported personal reaction alone.
items.expected_evidence: avoid collapsing ethical analysis into unsupported personal reaction alone
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner preserves avoid collapsing ethical analysis into unsupported personal reaction alone in a changed computing context or representation.

items.prompt: After a delay, the learner is given a new communication, attribution, impact, or governance case and must justify the responsible choice. Show how they would identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways.
items.expected_evidence: identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner still demonstrates identify who is affected by a bounded computing system and in what ways on a delayed recheck without a blocking recurrence.

items.prompt: Probe computing impacts, bias, and human-centered evaluation through a short audience, impact, or systems case before closing ethics, governance, and societal consequences of computing systems, and decide whether the current issue is really upstream.
items.expected_evidence: shows whether computing impacts, bias, and human-centered evaluation is secure enough to support ethics, governance, and societal consequences of computing systems
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner response makes it possible to distinguish an upstream gap in computing impacts, bias, and human-centered evaluation from a current-node issue in ethics, governance, and societal consequences of computing systems.
items.suspected_knowledge_gap_if_incorrect: computing impacts, bias, and human-centered evaluation

items.prompt: Probe systems architecture, resource constraints, and platform layers through a short audience, impact, or systems case before closing ethics, governance, and societal consequences of computing systems, and decide whether the current issue is really upstream.
items.expected_evidence: shows whether systems architecture, resource constraints, and platform layers is secure enough to support ethics, governance, and societal consequences of computing systems
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner response makes it possible to distinguish an upstream gap in systems architecture, resource constraints, and platform layers from a current-node issue in ethics, governance, and societal consequences of computing systems.
items.suspected_knowledge_gap_if_incorrect: systems architecture, resource constraints, and platform layers
